planting.note: A day-neutral purple sprouting hybrid grown as a quick spring or fall crop rather than overwintered.

directions: Transplant seedlings into fertile soil for a fast crop of purple spears without needing to overwinter. Santee produces numerous slender, tender shoots that turn green when cooked. Harvest the spears while their buds are closed, cutting often to keep new ones forming.

planting.note: A baby-broccoli type grown for a long harvest of slim, tender stems.

directions: Transplant into rich soil and remove the first small central head early to trigger heavy side-shoot production. Apollo yields long, slender stems with small florets that stay sweet and tender. Keep cutting shoots every few days while the buds are still closed to prolong the harvest.

planting.note: The original tenderstem broccolini hybrid, grown for repeated cuttings of sweet stems.

directions: Set transplants into fertile soil and pinch out the first small head to push the plant into shoot production. Aspabroc then delivers a steady flow of long, tender stems topped with tiny florets. Harvest shoots young and frequently, snapping them while the buds remain tight for the best flavor.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. botrytis 'Snowball Y Improved'

planting.note: A classic self-blanching heirloom whose leaves curl over the developing curd.

directions: Transplant into rich, consistently moist soil and avoid any check to growth that could cause small buttons. As the white curd forms, tie the outer leaves over it to keep the head pale and tender. Cut the head while it is firm and tight, before the curd starts to separate into ricey segments.

planting.note: An early, vigorous hybrid that is more forgiving of variable weather than most cauliflower.

directions: Set out transplants into fertile ground and keep water and nutrients steady for smooth curd development. Snow Crown matures quickly and tolerates a bit more stress than older varieties. Blanch by folding leaves over the head, then cut once the white curd is full and still tightly beaded.

planting.note: A self-wrapping variety adaptable to both spring and fall, with good heat and cold tolerance.

directions: Transplant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep growth uninterrupted for large curds. Amazing wraps its own leaves over the head and tolerates a wider range of temperatures than many cauliflowers. Harvest the dense white head while it stays firm and before the curd loosens.

planting.note: An orange hybrid whose color intensifies with sun and deepens further when cooked.

directions: Transplant into rich soil and, unlike white types, leave the head exposed to sunlight so its orange color develops fully. Keep moisture even to encourage a smooth, dense curd rich in beta-carotene. Cut the vivid orange head while it is firm and tight, before the beads begin to spread.

planting.note: An easy-growing orange hybrid with soft color and good tolerance of variable conditions.

directions: Set transplants into fertile soil and let the developing curd catch sunlight to build its pale-orange hue. Flame Star is more forgiving than many colored types and produces a smooth, dome-shaped head. Harvest while the curd remains firm and tightly packed.

planting.note: A striking purple hybrid whose color needs sun exposure and does not require blanching.

directions: Transplant into rich soil and leave the head fully exposed so the purple pigment develops its deepest color. Keep watering consistent to prevent stress that can dull the shade or cause loose curds. Cut the vivid violet head while firm, knowing the color softens toward green when cooked.

planting.note: A bright purple hybrid that colors best with strong sun and cool finishing weather.

directions: Transplant into fertile ground and let the curd receive full sun to intensify its purple tone. Steady moisture and cool nights during head formation produce the most vivid color. Harvest the domed purple head while the beads stay tight and compact.

planting.note: A green-curded hybrid with a milder, sweeter flavor than white cauliflower.

directions: Transplant into rich soil and leave the head uncovered, as the green color comes from sunlight and needs no blanching. Keep the plants growing steadily with even water and feeding. Cut the lime-green head while it is firm and tight, before the curd begins to separate.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. botrytis 'Violetto di Sicilia'

planting.note: An Italian heirloom with large purple heads, best grown as a long-season fall crop.

directions: Transplant into deep, fertile soil and give this vigorous heirloom plenty of room and a long, cool growing season. Leave the head exposed to sun so the purple color develops fully across the large curd. Harvest while firm, and expect the violet shade to turn green during cooking.

planting.note: A reliable white hybrid producing heavy, deep, well-protected curds.

directions: Transplant into rich, moist soil and keep growth steady to build a large, dense head. As the curd forms, gather the leaves over it to keep the head clean and white. Cut when firm and full-sized, before the segments loosen or become grainy.

planting.note: A well-wrapped hybrid favored for late summer and fall harvests.

directions: Set transplants into fertile soil and keep them growing without stress for uniform curds. Attribute wraps its own leaves well, keeping the white head naturally protected as it matures. Harvest the firm, heavy head before the curd begins to open.

planting.note: A well-adapted white hybrid that self-wraps and holds in the field.

directions: Transplant into rich, moist soil and maintain steady growth for smooth, bright curds. Mardi covers its head with wrapping leaves and stands well without bolting under mild stress. Cut the head while it is firm and tight, before the beads separate.

planting.note: An open-pollinated self-blanching variety with dependable white heads.

directions: Transplant into fertile, evenly watered soil and avoid checks that lead to premature buttoning. Igloo's leaves fold naturally over the head to keep it white with little extra effort. Harvest the compact head while it is still firm and closed.

planting.note: A loose-curd cauliflower grown for long, edible florets on slender white stems.

directions: Transplant into fertile soil and grow like standard cauliflower, but expect an airy, open head rather than a solid dome. The long, tender stems and small florets are harvested together and need no blanching. Cut the stems when they reach a usable length and the tiny buds are still closed.

planting.note: An Italian heirloom romanesco best grown as a long-season fall crop for its spiraled lime-green head.

directions: Transplant into deep, rich soil and give this vigorous plant a long, cool stretch to develop its intricate head. The chartreuse curd forms a pattern of repeating pointed spirals and needs no blanching. Harvest while the head is firm and the fractal cones are still tight and crisp.

planting.note: A uniform romanesco hybrid producing well-formed apple-green spiraled heads.

directions: Transplant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep growth steady through a cool finishing season. Veronica forms a symmetrical head of pointed green spirals and does not require blanching. Cut the head while firm and the cones are sharply defined, before the curd loosens.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. gemmifera 'Long Island Improved'

planting.note: Start indoors in early summer so plants mature into cool fall weather.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep indoors and set out sturdy transplants once they have several true leaves. Keep the soil evenly moist and side-dress with nitrogen mid-season to fuel the tall stalks. Snap sprouts from the bottom of the stem first, once they are firm and about an inch across; flavor sweetens after a light frost.

planting.note: This early hybrid can be set out in summer for a dependable fall harvest.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant compact, dark-green seedlings into firm, fertile ground. Stake plants in windy sites since the heavy stalks can lodge. Harvest the tight, uniform sprouts from the base upward as they reach full size.

planting.note: A late hybrid best started in early summer for a long fall picking window.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set them deep into rich soil once nights begin to cool. Water steadily through summer to prevent loose, open sprouts. Pick the heavy, dark sprouts over several weeks, working from the bottom of the stalk upward.

planting.note: An early, adaptable hybrid for both spring and fall transplanting.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors and transplant healthy seedlings into firmed, well-limed soil. Maintain consistent moisture and remove yellowing lower leaves to improve airflow. Begin harvesting once the lowest sprouts are firm and marble to walnut sized.

planting.note: A purple-red heirloom-type sprout for fall harvest after cool weather deepens its color.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out young plants into fertile, firm soil. Cool autumn nights intensify the red-purple coloring of the sprouts. Harvest from the base of the stalk upward once the sprouts are firm; the color softens somewhat when cooked.

planting.note: A vigorous purple hybrid started in early summer for a late fall crop.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and plant them deeply into rich, well-drained ground. These tall plants benefit from staking as the sprouts fill in. Pick the firm purple sprouts progressively up the stalk after the first frosts have arrived.

planting.note: An upright hybrid that holds its sprouts well for fall and early winter picking.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant strong seedlings into firm, fertile soil. Keep watering even through the growing season to build dense, well-filled sprouts. Harvest the tight sprouts from the bottom up over an extended period once they firm up.

planting.note: A reliable green hybrid suited to fall harvest in most regions.

directions: Start seed shallowly indoors and move sturdy transplants into firmed soil. Feed and water consistently to keep sprouts tight rather than loose and leafy. Begin harvesting when the lowest sprouts are firm and about an inch wide.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. gongylodes 'Early White Vienna'

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half inch deep and thin seedlings to give each bulb room to swell. Water steadily so the pale green bulbs form quickly and stay tender. Harvest when the swollen stems reach two to three inches across, before they turn woody.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. gongylodes 'Early Purple Vienna'

planting.note: A purple-skinned heirloom for spring and fall sowings in cool weather.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin plants once they are a few inches tall. The skin is purple but the crisp flesh inside stays white and mild. Pull bulbs when they are golf-ball to tennis-ball size for the sweetest, most tender eating.

planting.note: A vivid purple hybrid that resists splitting better than older types.

directions: Sow seed directly and thin to even spacing for uniform bulbs. Consistent moisture keeps the purple bulbs smooth and prevents cracking. Harvest at two to three inches across while the interior is still crisp and sweet.

planting.note: A giant storage hybrid that stays tender even at large size.

directions: Direct sow and thin generously since these bulbs grow far larger than standard kohlrabi. Unlike most types, the flesh stays crisp and sweet even when the bulb reaches softball size or bigger. Harvest through fall and store the solid bulbs in a cool cellar for winter use.

planting.note: A pale green giant heirloom prized for staying tender at large size.

directions: Sow directly and space widely to let the huge bulbs develop fully. This variety can swell to eight inches or more across without turning fibrous. Harvest anytime once bulbs are usable size; the mild white flesh keeps well in cool storage.

planting.note: An early deep-purple variety good for quick spring and fall crops.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin early to keep bulbs from crowding. Keep the soil moist so the deep purple bulbs size up fast and stay crisp. Harvest young at two to three inches across for the best texture.

planting.note: Fast-growing rapini for repeated spring and fall sowings in cool weather.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half inch deep in cool soil and thin lightly. Grow quickly with steady water since heat and stress push it to bolt. Cut the whole plant when small flower buds form but before the yellow flowers open, harvesting leaves, stems, and buds together.

planting.note: An early rapini selection well suited to quick spring plantings.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil and keep the bed evenly moist for fast growth. Succession sow every couple of weeks for a steady supply of tender shoots. Harvest the leafy stems and buds just as the small florets appear, before the flowers open.

planting.note: A quick forty-day-type rapini for spring and fall cutting.

directions: Sow shallowly into cool, fertile soil and thin to reduce crowding. Rapid, unchecked growth gives the mildest, most tender stems. Cut plants at the base when buds are formed but tightly closed, and expect flavorful side shoots afterward.

planting.note: A tender hybrid Chinese broccoli tolerant of a wider temperature range than most.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half inch deep and thin plants to give each room to branch. Water regularly to keep the thick stems crisp and mild. Cut the main stem with its cluster of buds when they are just beginning to open, then harvest the side shoots that follow.

planting.note: A traditional Chinese broccoli grown for its sweet stems, leaves, and buds.

directions: Direct sow into fertile soil and thin seedlings as they establish. Steady moisture and cool conditions produce the tenderest stalks and mildest flavor. Harvest by cutting the flowering stems when the buds show but before the small white flowers fully open.

planting.note: An early, uniform Chinese broccoli that produces slender tender stems quickly.

directions: Sow directly and thin to allow airflow between plants. Grow with consistent water for smooth, sweet stalks and abundant side shoots. Cut the central stem as its buds begin to swell, then keep harvesting the branching shoots that form.

planting.note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer, avoiding the heat of midsummer which triggers bolting.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in fertile, evenly moist soil and thin seedlings once they have a couple of true leaves. Keep the bed consistently watered to prevent bitterness and premature flowering. Cut whole heads when the thick white stalks are firm and full, usually before hot weather arrives.

common_name: Mei Qing Choi Baby Pak Choi
scientific_name: Brassica rapa var. chinensis 'Mei Qing Choi'

planting.note: A slow-bolting baby type that suits spring and fall succession sowings.

directions: Plant seed shallowly and keep soil moist for even germination and quick, tender growth. Its pale green stems resist bolting better than most, so it tolerates a slightly wider planting window. Harvest the compact heads young while the leaves are crisp and mild.

planting.note: A tiny single-serving type ideal for close spacing and quick turnover.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in blocks or short rows and keep the soil damp. Because the plants stay small, they mature fast and can be grown at high density. Pull the whole miniature heads once they reach three to five inches tall.

planting.note: Purple-red leaf color deepens in the cool weather of spring and fall.

directions: Sow shallowly in rich soil and thin to give each plant room to color up. Cool temperatures and strong light intensify the reddish-purple leaf tops, so spring and autumn crops show the best color. Harvest as baby leaves for salad or grow on to small heads.

planting.note: Green-stemmed soup-spoon type widely grown across East Asia.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and keep the bed evenly moist to encourage tender, spoon-shaped stalks. Feed lightly for fast growth and harvest before hot weather causes bolting. Cut heads at the base when the pale green petioles are plump and juicy.

planting.note: More heat and bolt tolerant than most, extending the sowing season.

directions: Sow shallowly in fertile soil and keep well watered during warm spells. Its improved bolt resistance lets you plant later into spring and earlier in late summer than standard pak choi. Harvest firm dark-green heads while the stems are still crisp.

planting.note: A vigorous full-size white-stemmed hybrid with good uniformity.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin to full spacing so heads size up evenly. Consistent moisture and modest feeding produce heavy, upright plants. Cut whole heads when the broad white stalks feel firm and full.

planting.note: Deep purple leaves make a colorful baby-leaf or salad crop.

directions: Sow thinly in cool weather for the deepest purple pigment, which fades in summer heat. Keep evenly watered and harvest young leaves for salads or let plants grow to small heads. Best flavor and color come from spring and fall crops.

planting.note: Best as a fall crop; sow mid to late summer so heads mature in cool weather.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to a foot apart, since crowded plants form loose heads. This tall cylindrical type is prone to bolting, so grow it into cooling fall weather rather than spring heat. Harvest the elongated heads when they feel dense and solid.

planting.note: A disease-tolerant barrel type; start indoors and set out for a fall harvest.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant carefully to avoid stressing the roots, which can trigger bolting. Keep plants steadily watered and fed for tight barrel-shaped heads. Cut when heads are firm and heavy, ideally after light frosts sweeten the flavor.

planting.note: One of the more bolt-tolerant napas, allowing a careful spring sowing too.

directions: Start indoors or direct sow, thinning to give each plant ample room. Its improved bolt resistance makes it one of the safer choices for an early crop, though fall is still most reliable. Harvest solid, well-filled heads before hard freezes.

planting.note: A dependable large-headed fall hybrid with good field holding.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant into rich, moisture-retentive soil. Provide steady water so heads fill without splitting, and side-dress with nitrogen for size. Cut the dense heads once they resist a firm squeeze, holding well in the field into cool weather.

planting.note: A compact mini-napa that suits small gardens and tight spacing.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out young plants with minimal root disturbance. Its small size lets you space plants closer and harvest sooner than standard napa. Cut the compact, oval heads when firm, usually in cool early-fall weather.

planting.note: An upright, larger cousin of tatsoi that is very cold tolerant.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin to give the large savoyed leaves room to expand. It stands more upright than tatsoi and produces abundant thick, dark-green leaves. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head, best after cool weather sets in.

planting.note: Fast, feathery salad green that regrows well after cutting.

directions: Sow thickly for baby leaf or thin for larger bunches, planting a quarter inch deep. The deeply cut, feathery leaves have a mild peppery bite and tolerate both cold and modest heat. Cut leaves an inch above the crown and plants will resprout for several harvests.

planting.note: A red-purple mizuna that holds color better than older red types.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist for quick, tender growth. The serrated leaves carry deep red-purple color that stays vivid even in warmer conditions, adding contrast to salad mixes. Harvest as baby leaves or cut whole for repeated regrowth.

planting.note: The smooth strap-leaved relative of mizuna, milder in flavor.

directions: Direct sow in rows and keep evenly watered for a steady cut-and-come-again crop. Unlike frilly mizuna, mibuna has long narrow entire leaves with a gentle mustardy taste. Snip leaves regularly and the clump will keep producing through cool weather.

planting.note: Fast, forgiving Japanese mustard spinach for spring through fall.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin for baby or full-size leaves as desired. This vigorous green tolerates heat and cold better than most brassicas, giving a long harvest window. Pick outer leaves continually or cut the whole plant while the glossy leaves are tender.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant hybrid komatsuna that can be sown even in summer.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and keep the bed moist during warm weather. Its strong bolt and heat resistance make it one of the few tender greens that thrives in summer plantings. Harvest young leaves for salad or grown-on leaves for cooking.

planting.note: A classic Southern heirloom mustard for spring and fall greens.

directions: Sow a quarter to half inch deep in fertile soil and thin to prevent crowding. The large bright-green leaves have frilled edges and a pungent flavor that mellows with cooking or frost. Harvest outer leaves young for salad or let plants bulk up for a cooked-greens crop.

planting.note: Frilly bright-green mustard that is slow to bolt and very pungent.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and keep moist for tender, ruffled leaves. This variety resists bolting well, extending the harvest of its curly, hot-flavored foliage. Pick baby leaves for a spicy salad accent or larger leaves for cooking.

planting.note: Japanese mustard with rounded purple-veined leaves; sweeter in cold weather.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin for the size you want. The broad rounded leaves show purple tints and bold veining that deepen in cool weather, when flavor also turns milder and sweeter. Harvest baby leaves for salad or mature leaves for stir-fry and pickles.

planting.note: Finely serrated chartreuse mustard prized for salad mixes.

directions: Sow thickly for baby leaf and keep the soil moist. The lacy, bright yellow-green leaves add texture and a spicy kick to cut salad blends. Harvest young by cutting an inch above the crown, and plants will regrow for another picking.

planting.note: Wispy dark-red serrated mustard that adds color and bite to salads.

directions: Sow shallowly and thickly for a baby-leaf cut. The finely dissected leaves are deep burgundy over green and carry a mild mustard heat. Cut young for salad mixes and expect vigorous regrowth in cool weather.

planting.note: Finely cut red-purple mustard similar to ruby streaks but more serrated.

directions: Direct sow thinly to thickly depending on whether you want leaves or bunches. The lacy, deeply toothed leaves flush reddish-purple and bring a hot mustard flavor to salad mixes. Harvest young and repeatedly for the tenderest, mildest greens.

planting.note: Fast, large-leaved dark-red mustard with smooth foliage.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to preferred size. The broad, glossy leaves are a deep garnet color and grow quickly for both baby leaf and full-size use. Its mild early flavor sharpens with age, so harvest younger leaves for salads.

planting.note: Bright-green frilly mustard with a sharp horseradish-like heat.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep evenly watered for tender leaves. Named for its wasabi-like bite, its ruffled chartreuse leaves are hottest when grown fast in cool weather. Pick baby leaves for a pungent salad accent or larger leaves for cooking.

planting.note: Exceptionally cold-hardy mustard that can overwinter in mild areas.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep in late summer or early spring. This hardy mustard tolerates hard frosts and keeps producing sharp-flavored leaves when little else grows. Harvest outer leaves as needed through cold weather; the plant regrows readily.

planting.note: A quick, heat- and cold-tolerant green milder than true mustards.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin as plants grow. Sometimes called komatsuna, it produces smooth dark-green leaves with a mild spinach-mustard flavor and shrugs off both heat and cold. Harvest young leaves for salad or full leaves for cooking over a long season.

planting.note: A loose-leaf non-heading Chinese cabbage with a mild, sweet flavor.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin for baby leaf or full loose heads. The ruffled, pale chartreuse leaves and crisp white stems are tender and sweet, good raw or cooked. Harvest young leaves for salad or cut whole plants before hot weather causes bolting.

planting.note: A fast loose-leaf brassica combining pak choi stems and mild leaves.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist for quick, tender growth. This non-heading green offers flat pale-green leaves and juicy white stems with a sweet, mild taste. Harvest baby leaves for salad or whole plants while young, before bolting in heat.

planting.note: A komatsuna-cabbage cross with large sweet leaves and good hardiness.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin generously, as plants grow large. This hybrid produces big, round, tender leaves with a mild sweet flavor and stands up well to cold. Harvest outer leaves continually or cut the whole plant, and it will regrow from the crown.

planting.note: Vigorous mustard tolerant of both heat and cold, slow to bolt.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin as plants develop. This robust mustard produces smooth green leaves with a mild flavor that combines cabbage and mustard notes, and it resists bolting in warm spells. Harvest young leaves for salad or larger leaves for cooking across a long season.

scientific_name: Brassica rapa subsp. rapa 'Purple Top White Globe'

planting.note: Sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed about a half inch deep in loosened soil and thin seedlings to a few inches apart once true leaves appear. Keep moisture even so the globe-shaped roots swell without cracking or turning woody. Pull when roots reach two to three inches across, with the shoulders showing their signature purple blush above white flesh.

planting.note: Best grown as a fall crop where light frost sweetens the roots.

directions: Scatter seed thinly and cover lightly, thinning to give each root room to round out. Consistent water keeps the amber flesh tender and mild rather than fibrous. Harvest the small golden globes at two to three inches; they store well and stay sweet through cool weather.

planting.note: Sow in succession every two weeks in spring and fall for a steady supply.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin early, since crowding slows the smooth white roots. Grow quickly with steady moisture to keep the flavor sweet enough to eat raw. Harvest the tender roots small, around one to two inches, and enjoy the mild greens as a bonus.

planting.note: Quick and uniform, ideal for repeated sowings through the cool season.

directions: Direct sow about a half inch deep and thin promptly for even, round white roots. This hybrid resists getting pithy, so it holds well in the ground longer than most. Begin pulling at golf-ball size and continue as roots enlarge without loss of quality.

planting.note: An old heirloom that performs in both spring and fall plantings.

directions: Sow thinly in fertile soil and thin to a few inches so the egg-shaped roots can form. Keep the bed evenly moist to keep the smooth white flesh crisp and sweet. Harvest at two to three inches, before the roots grow large enough to turn strong-flavored.

planting.note: The red-skinned roots and red stems add color to spring and fall beds.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin for room, growing the plants quickly with even water. The flat, bright red roots have crisp white flesh best eaten before they oversize. Pull at about two inches for the sweetest, most tender eating.

planting.note: Grow for tender greens early and let some roots swell for later use.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin, harvesting some plants young for the abundant, mild leaves. Left to mature, the roots round into large white globes with sweet flesh. Cut greens as needed and dig roots at three to four inches for cooking.

planting.note: Grown for greens rather than roots; sow densely for cut-and-come-again harvests.

directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed thickly, since this variety makes leaves rather than usable roots. Keep the soil moist to encourage lush, dark green foliage. Cut the outer leaves once they reach several inches, and the plant will regrow for repeated pickings.

planting.note: A fall-oriented hybrid grown for both greens and smooth white roots.

directions: Sow in late summer for fall harvest, covering seed lightly and thinning as plants grow. The vigorous tops can be cut for greens while the roots continue to size up. Dig the pure white globes once they reach three to four inches across.

planting.note: Very fast white salad turnip for close, frequent sowings.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin early so the quick roots stay smooth and round. Grow fast with steady water for sweet, crisp flesh mild enough to eat raw. Pull at one to two inches while the skin is still glossy and tender.

planting.note: An early white heirloom suited to both spring and autumn sowings.

directions: Sow thinly about a half inch deep and thin seedlings to give the roots space. Even moisture keeps the pure white flesh fine-grained and mild. Harvest at two to three inches, when the small globes are at their most tender.

scientific_name: Brassica napus var. napobrassica 'American Purple Top'

planting.note: Sow in mid to late summer so the large roots mature into cool fall weather.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep and thin generously, since rutabagas need room to bulk up. Water steadily through the long season to prevent woody, cracked roots. Harvest after light frosts, when the purple-shouldered yellow roots reach four to five inches and taste sweetest.

planting.note: A reliable storage standard sown in summer for autumn harvest.

directions: Direct sow and thin to at least six inches so each root can reach full size. Keep moisture consistent to build smooth, uniform globes with fine yellow flesh. Dig after the first frosts and cure briefly; the roots store for months in cool conditions.

planting.note: A smooth-skinned selection with good uniformity for fall crops.

directions: Sow in summer and thin early so the round roots develop evenly. Steady watering keeps the yellow flesh dense and mild rather than fibrous. Harvest in fall after a frost or two has sweetened the sugars.

planting.note: A purple-topped variety valued for storage quality.

directions: Sow a half inch deep in loose, fertile soil and thin to give each plant room. Water evenly and mulch to keep roots developing steadily through late summer. Lift the purple-shouldered roots after frost, then store them cool and humid for winter use.

planting.note: A hardy green-topped German heirloom bred for winter keeping.

directions: Sow in mid-summer and thin well so the sturdy roots can size up fully. Consistent moisture and cool nights build dense, sweet yellow flesh. Harvest after frost and store the roots in a cool cellar, where they hold through winter.

planting.note: A Vermont heirloom sold as a turnip but botanically a rutabaga.

directions: Sow in summer and thin to give the tapered white roots space to grow. Keep watering even so the mild, creamy flesh stays tender. Harvest after frost, which brings out the sweet, delicate flavor this heirloom is prized for.

planting.note: Sow in succession every week or two through the cool season.

directions: Sow seed about a half inch deep and thin to an inch so the roots can round out. Keep the soil evenly moist and grow quickly to avoid pithy, hot roots. Pull the bright red globes at about one inch, while the crisp white flesh is still mild.

planting.note: Large round red radish that holds its quality longer than most.

directions: Sow a half inch deep and thin so the big globes have room to fill out. Keep moisture even, as this variety resists getting pithy if left slightly longer. Pull the round scarlet roots at about one and a half inches for crisp, mild flesh.

planting.note: A bicolor round radish, red on top with a white tip.

directions: Sow thinly and thin to an inch apart for well-shaped roots. Fast, even growth keeps the crisp white flesh sweet and mild. Harvest at about one inch, when the red-and-white roots are firm and tender.

planting.note: A long white heirloom radish tolerant of a wider range of conditions.

directions: Sow in deep, loose soil so the tapering white roots grow straight. Keep watering steady to hold the crisp, mildly pungent flesh. Pull the slender roots at four to five inches long, before they become fibrous.

planting.note: A colorful blend producing red, purple, pink, and white roots.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to space the mixed-color roots. Grow quickly with even moisture for crisp, mild flesh across the color range. Harvest at about one inch, pulling the rainbow of round roots as they size up.

planting.note: An heirloom that sizes larger than most spring radishes without turning pithy.

directions: Sow a half inch deep and thin a bit wider than usual to accommodate the large roots. Keep soil moisture even so the crimson globes stay crisp as they enlarge. Pull at one and a half to two inches, when the white flesh is still firm and mild.

planting.note: A bright purple round radish that holds crispness well.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to an inch for evenly rounded roots. Grow fast with steady water to keep the translucent white interior sweet and crunchy. Harvest at about one inch, when the purple skin is deep and glossy.

planting.note: An old winter radish sown in late summer for fall storage.

directions: Sow in late summer in deep soil and thin so the roots can lengthen. Water steadily through fall for firm, spicy flesh. Harvest the rose-pink cylindrical roots at five to six inches, and store the surplus cool for winter.

planting.note: Sow in late summer so the roots mature in cool weather for best color.

directions: Sow in loose soil and thin generously to let the large round roots develop. Steady cool-weather growth deepens the pink interior and keeps the flavor mild and sweet. Harvest at three to four inches, when the pale green shoulders hint at the rosy flesh inside.

planting.note: A pungent black-skinned winter radish for fall harvest and storage.

directions: Sow in late summer and thin well so the roots can bulk into full globes. Keep moisture even for dense white flesh under the rough black skin. Harvest at three to four inches; the roots keep for months in cool, humid storage.

planting.note: An unusual yellow-skinned round radish from Poland.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to an inch for well-rounded roots. Even moisture and quick growth keep the white flesh crisp and mildly spicy. Harvest the matte yellow globes at about one inch across.

planting.note: A rosy-red cylindrical Asian radish good for spring or fall.

directions: Sow in loose soil and thin so the smooth pink roots can elongate. Grow steadily with even water for crisp white flesh with a hint of heat. Harvest at four to five inches; the tops are also good cooked as greens.

planting.note: Grown for its edible seed pods rather than roots, and tolerant of heat.

directions: Sow after frost and let the tall plants flower, since the crop is the slender pods, not the root. Pick the pods young and crisp, while they snap cleanly and taste mildly peppery. Keep harvesting regularly to encourage the plant to keep setting new pods.

planting.note: A classic pickling daikon sown in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Sow in deep, loose soil so the long white roots grow straight and unforked. Thin to several inches and water steadily to prevent pithy centers. Harvest the cylindrical roots at twelve to sixteen inches, before hard freezes; they hold well in cool storage.

scientific_name: Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus 'Minowase Summer Cross'

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid daikon more tolerant of warm sowing conditions.

directions: Sow in deep, well-worked soil and thin so the roots can lengthen freely. Keep moisture even to grow long, crisp, mild white roots. Harvest at twelve to eighteen inches, pulling before the roots become fibrous.

planting.note: A bolt-resistant hybrid that can be sown in spring as well as fall.

directions: Sow in deep soil and thin to give the long white roots room to form. Its strong bolt resistance lets it hold in the ground even as days lengthen. Harvest the smooth roots at twelve to eighteen inches while the flesh is crisp and mild.

planting.note: A shorter-rooted hybrid daikon suited to fall sowing.

directions: Sow in loosened soil and thin so the roots develop cleanly. Keep watering even to grow crisp, white, mild-flavored roots. Harvest at ten to fourteen inches, before hard frost sets in.

planting.note: A purple-skinned daikon also used as a soil-building cover crop.

directions: Sow in deep soil and thin for straight roots, or broadcast thickly as a fall cover crop. Even moisture builds crisp flesh tinged purple near the skin. Harvest the roots at eight to twelve inches for eating, or leave them to winterkill and enrich the soil.

planting.note: A giant round Japanese heirloom needing a long, cool growing season.

directions: Sow in very deep, rich soil and thin widely, since the roots can grow enormous. Water steadily over the long season to build firm, sweet white flesh. Harvest the huge round roots in cool fall weather once they reach full size.

planting.note: The standard long white daikon widely grown across East Asia.

directions: Sow in deep, stone-free soil so the long roots grow straight and smooth. Thin to several inches and keep moisture even to avoid splitting or hollow centers. Harvest at twelve to eighteen inches for crisp, mild flesh good raw, cooked, or pickled.

planting.note: A green-fleshed Chinese radish sown in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Sow in loose soil and thin so the roots can size up evenly. Cool-weather growth develops the crisp green flesh and mildly sweet flavor. Harvest at four to six inches, when the green shoulders are well colored; the roots store well.

planting.note: A hybrid round black radish with uniform roots for fall storage.

directions: Sow in late summer and thin to give the round roots room to form. Even moisture builds firm white flesh under the black skin. Harvest at three to four inches; the dense roots keep for months when stored cool.

planting.note: A soft pink round radish for spring and fall sowings.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to an inch so the roots round out evenly. Steady water and quick growth keep the white flesh crisp and mild. Harvest the rose-pink globes at about one inch, while firm and tender.

planting.note: A Polish variety valued for sweet flesh and good winter storage.

directions: Sow in summer and thin generously so the roots can bulk up fully. Keep moisture consistent through the season for dense, sweet yellow flesh. Harvest after frost and cure the purple-topped roots for long winter keeping.

planting.note: Direct sow in loose soil as soon as it can be worked in early spring, and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in deeply worked, stone-free soil and keep the surface evenly moist until sprouts appear. Thin seedlings to two or three inches apart once the tops are a couple inches tall so roots have room to fill out. Pull when the blunt-tipped roots reach six to seven inches long and color up bright orange.

planting.note: Sow early for a tender first-of-season Nantes hybrid.

directions: Scatter seed thinly in shallow furrows and firm the soil gently to hold moisture around the fine seed. Water consistently so the young roots size up quickly without splitting, and thin to about two inches. This early hybrid is sweet and best harvested young at five to six inches.

planting.note: A vigorous Nantes hybrid well suited to a main-season or fall storage sowing.

directions: Sow into a fine, deeply loosened bed and keep evenly damp through the slow germination period. Its healthy tops resist leaf diseases, so this variety holds well for late lifting and winter storage. Harvest roots at seven to eight inches once fully colored, or leave in the ground under mulch to sweeten.

planting.note: Fast, cold-tolerant Nantes hybrid good for very early and overwintered sowings.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil and keep the seedbed moist; this variety germinates and grows well when temperatures are still low. Thin to two inches and water evenly to keep the smooth cylindrical roots sweet. Lift young for baby carrots or let mature to six or seven inches.

planting.note: A very sweet, slender Nantes-type hybrid for early and successional sowings.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in fine-textured soil and mist the bed until the delicate ferny tops emerge. Because the roots are thin and tender they are excellent pulled young as a snacking carrot. Keep moisture steady to avoid cracking and harvest as soon as roots reach finger to five-inch size.

planting.note: Uniform, blunt-tipped Nantes hybrid that performs across a range of soils.

directions: Sow thinly a quarter inch deep and keep the surface from crusting so seedlings break through easily. Thin to two inches for well-shaped, uniform roots. Its reliably sweet flavor is at its best when harvested at six inches, and it holds in the ground without turning woody.

scientific_name: Daucus carota subsp. sativus 'Nantes Half Long'

planting.note: Open-pollinated Nantes with a barely-there core and rounded tip.

directions: Work the bed to a fine crumb at least a foot deep and sow the small seed shallowly in rows. Keep the seedbed evenly moist through germination and thin to two or three inches. The nearly coreless roots are crisp and sweet; harvest at six to seven inches for the best eating quality.

planting.note: A slender, high-sugar Nantes hybrid prized for snacking.

directions: Sow seed thinly and keep the ground damp until the ferny seedlings appear. Thin to about two inches and water steadily for tender, sugary roots. These thin carrots are delicious eaten fresh; pull them young once they reach five to six inches.

scientific_name: Daucus carota subsp. sativus 'Red Cored Chantenay'

planting.note: A stout, broad-shouldered heirloom that tolerates heavier ground.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in rows and keep moist through the slow germination stage. Because the roots are short and wedge-shaped they handle clay and shallow soils better than long types, so thin generously to three inches. Harvest the thick five-to-seven-inch roots for fresh use, juicing, and winter storage.

planting.note: A vigorous Chantenay-type hybrid with strong, disease-resistant tops.

directions: Direct sow into loosened soil and keep the bed damp until seedlings establish. Its sturdy tops make the roots easy to pull, and the blocky shape resists breaking. Lift when roots reach five to six inches for good storage and roasting quality.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant Chantenay-type popular in warm climates and fall crops.

directions: Sow in fertile, deeply worked soil and shade the seedbed lightly if temperatures are high during germination. This variety keeps sizing up in warm weather where other carrots bolt, so it suits late sowings. Harvest the thick, tapered roots at six to eight inches.

planting.note: An improved Kuroda selection with smooth, deep-orange roots for warm regions.

directions: Sow shallowly in rich, loose soil and water regularly through warm spells to prevent tough roots. It performs where summers are hot and is a dependable choice for a fall harvest. Pull the stout, sweet roots once they reach six to eight inches.

planting.note: The classic long, tapered market carrot that needs deep, loose soil.

directions: These long roots only form well in deep, sandy or well-worked loam free of stones, so cultivate at least a foot down before sowing. Sow shallowly, keep evenly moist, and thin to two or three inches. Harvest the slim tapering roots at eight to nine inches once richly colored.

planting.note: A sweet Imperator-type hybrid high in carotene.

directions: Sow into deep, loosened soil so the long slender roots can grow straight. Keep the bed watered evenly to keep the roots tender and crack-free. These extra-sweet carrots are good raw or juiced; harvest at eight to nine inches.

planting.note: A long, coreless Imperator-type heirloom bred for sweetness.

directions: Prepare a deep, stone-free bed and sow the fine seed a quarter inch down. Water consistently as the tapering roots lengthen so they stay tender and juicy. Harvest at eight or nine inches; the nearly coreless flesh is excellent fresh, cooked, or juiced.

planting.note: A tough, heat-tolerant heirloom that adapts to heavier soils.

directions: Sow in rows a quarter inch deep and keep moist through the slow, uneven germination. This dependable variety tolerates clay and summer heat better than most, so thin to three inches and mulch to conserve moisture. Harvest the strong, tapered roots at six to seven inches for fresh use, canning, or storage.

scientific_name: Daucus carota subsp. sativus 'Danvers Half Long'

planting.note: A shorter, sturdy Danvers form for shallow or clay soils.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and firm the soil to hold moisture around the seed. The stout, gently tapering roots push through heavier ground more easily than long carrots, so thin to three inches. Lift at six inches for a versatile storage and cooking carrot.

planting.note: A striking hybrid with purple skin and bright orange interior.

directions: Sow in deeply worked soil and keep evenly moist for smooth, straight roots. The purple pigment fades with long cooking, so serve these raw or lightly cooked to show off the two-tone color. Harvest at seven to eight inches when the shoulders turn deep purple.

planting.note: An open-pollinated carrot with purple skin and orange-to-yellow flesh.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and keep the surface moist until the fine seedlings emerge. Thin to two or three inches so the tapering roots color fully. Pull at six to eight inches; the bright purple exterior and spicy-sweet flavor make a colorful addition to fresh plates.

planting.note: A red-fleshed carrot whose color deepens when cooked.

directions: Sow into loose, deep soil and water steadily for long, straight roots. The red pigment intensifies with cooking, so these are especially good roasted. Harvest at seven to nine inches once the roots have reddened through.

planting.note: A cheerful bright-yellow carrot with mild, sweet flavor.

directions: Sow shallowly in fine soil and keep the bed damp until seedlings appear. Thin to two or three inches for well-formed roots. These lemon-yellow carrots hold their color when cooked; harvest at six to seven inches.

planting.note: A creamy white carrot with mild, low-acid flavor.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and keep evenly moist for smooth roots. Thin to two or three inches so the pale roots develop fully. Harvest at six to eight inches; the tender white flesh is mild enough to please those who find orange carrots too strong.

planting.note: A smooth, sweet white hybrid with reliable uniformity.

directions: Sow into deep, loosened soil and water evenly for long, glossy roots. Thin to two or three inches for straight development. This hybrid is sweeter and more uniform than older white types; harvest at seven to eight inches.

planting.note: A tapered yellow carrot with good vigor and uniformity.

directions: Sow shallowly in a deeply worked bed and keep the seedbed moist through germination. Thin to two or three inches for the best-shaped roots. The lemon-yellow flesh stays colorful whether raw or roasted; harvest at seven to eight inches.

planting.note: A spicy-sweet carrot with reddish-purple skin and orange core.

directions: Direct sow in loose, deep soil and keep evenly damp for smooth roots. Thin to two or three inches so the tapering roots color fully. The purple skin gives way to a bright orange interior with a hint of spice; harvest at seven to eight inches.

planting.note: A hybrid that is deep purple all the way through the root.

directions: Sow into deeply loosened soil and water evenly for long, straight roots. Unlike two-tone purples, this variety is richly pigmented throughout, so it holds its color in cooking. Harvest at seven to nine inches once the roots are fully colored.

planting.note: A round French heirloom ideal for shallow soils and containers.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly in fine soil; because the roots are round they thrive in shallow beds, containers, and rocky ground. Keep moisture even and thin to about an inch and a half. Harvest the little globes when they reach the size of a large marble to a small radish.

planting.note: A round baby carrot bred for containers and heavy soils.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the surface damp until the seedlings emerge. These golf-ball-shaped roots grow well in pots and shallow or stony beds where long carrots fail. Thin to about an inch and a half and harvest when the roots reach one to one and a half inches across.

planting.note: A uniform round Parisian-type carrot for containers and market bunches.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly in loose soil and keep it moist through germination. The small round roots suit shallow beds and pots and mature quickly. Thin to about an inch and a half and harvest the sweet globes at one to one and a half inches across.

planting.note: A small Nantes-type baby carrot for early harvest and containers.

directions: Sow thinly in fine, loose soil and keep the bed evenly damp. These slim little carrots mature fast and do well in containers and shallow beds. Thin to about an inch and harvest when the roots reach three to four inches for tender baby carrots.

planting.note: A slender, quick early carrot good for cold-frame and first-of-season sowings.

directions: Sow early in cool soil, even under a cold frame or cloche, and keep the seedbed moist. The thin, fine-tipped roots mature quickly and are best eaten young. Thin to about an inch and a half and pull when the roots reach four to five inches.

planting.note: A large, late maincrop carrot that stands well into winter.

directions: Sow in late spring to early summer in deep, rich soil for a fall and winter crop. The robust roots grow large and hold their quality in the ground, so mulch heavily and lift as needed through cold weather. Harvest the thick, tapered roots at eight to ten inches.

planting.note: A long, cylindrical maincrop carrot excellent for winter storage.

directions: Sow in early summer in deeply worked soil and thin to three inches for full-size roots. The long, blunt cylindrical carrots store well through winter in the ground or in damp sand. Harvest at eight to ten inches before hard freezes, or mulch and lift as needed.

planting.note: A large French heirloom with long, smooth, tapering roots.

directions: Sow in deep, loose soil so the long tapering roots can grow straight and true. Keep moisture even and thin to three inches. This productive heirloom yields big, sweet roots; harvest at eight to ten inches for fresh use and storage.

planting.note: A fat, heart-shaped heirloom that thrives in heavy or shallow soil.

directions: Sow into rows and thin generously to three or four inches because these broad, wedge-shaped roots grow very stout. Their short, chunky form handles clay and stony ground where long carrots cannot. Harvest the wide-shouldered roots when they reach four to five inches across and up to six inches long.

planting.note: A North African heirloom that tolerates heat and dry conditions.

directions: Sow in warm soil and water steadily; this variety keeps growing in heat that stalls other carrots. Thin to three inches for smooth, tapered roots. Harvest at seven to nine inches for a sweet, aromatic carrot suited to warm-climate summers.

planting.note: A red carrot high in lycopene, best flavored when cooked.

directions: Sow in deep, loose soil and keep evenly watered for long, straight roots. The lycopene-rich flesh develops its best flavor and color when cooked, so use these roasted, steamed, or in sauces. Harvest at seven to nine inches.

planting.note: A hybrid mix that produces roots in orange, yellow, and white.

directions: Sow shallowly in deep, loose soil and keep the seedbed moist through germination. Thin to two or three inches so each colored root sizes up well. Harvest at six to eight inches to bring a mix of orange, yellow, and creamy-white carrots to the table.

planting.note: A deeply pigmented purple hybrid that is colorful throughout.

directions: Sow into a deep, fine bed and water evenly for smooth, straight roots. The intense purple runs right through the flesh and holds well in light cooking. Harvest at seven to eight inches when the shoulders are richly colored.

planting.note: A disease-resistant Nantes-type hybrid with strong, reliable yields.

directions: Sow in deeply worked soil and keep the seedbed evenly moist until seedlings appear. Its resistance to leaf blight and other diseases makes it dependable across a range of conditions, so thin to two or three inches. Harvest the smooth, sweet roots at seven to eight inches.

planting.note: Sow directly a few weeks before the last frost and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed clusters about half an inch deep and keep the soil evenly moist until the seedlings break ground. Thin the crowded shoots to one plant every three to four inches so the roots have room to swell. Pull the deep-red globes once they reach two to three inches across for the sweetest, most tender flesh.

planting.note: Sow in spring as soon as the ground can be worked, with successions into midsummer.

directions: Sow the seedballs about half an inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep them watered while they sprout. Thin to a single plant every three to four inches so each root can round out. Lift the beets young to enjoy the striking pink-and-white candy-stripe rings inside.

planting.note: Sow in cool spring soil; golden types can germinate a little slower than red ones.

directions: Press the seed clusters half an inch into well-worked soil and water gently to settle them. Thin the seedlings early so the golden roots can develop without crowding. Harvest at two to three inches for a mild, sweet flavor and flesh that does not bleed color onto the plate.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; a reliable golden variety with strong germination for its type.

directions: Sow the seed about half an inch deep and keep the bed consistently moist through emergence. Thin to one seedling every three to four inches so roots color up evenly. Pull the bright orange-gold globes while they are still smooth and tender for the best texture.

planting.note: Sow in spring; the long cylindrical roots grow partly above the soil line.

directions: Plant seed clusters half an inch deep in deeply worked, loose soil to give the long roots room to stretch. Thin carefully so each cylindrical beet can elongate without bending. Harvest when the roots are six to eight inches long, ideal for uniform slices.

planting.note: An early variety good for the first spring sowing and for abundant greens.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep as soon as the ground can be worked and keep it moist. Thin the seedlings to leave the vigorous, upright tops room to grow for cutting. Harvest the flattened-globe roots early, or take a first cutting of the lush greens while young.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; performs well even in less-than-ideal soils.

directions: Set the seed clusters half an inch deep and firm the soil lightly over them. Thin the young plants to a single beet every three to four inches. Harvest the smooth, deep-red globes at two to three inches for tender, sweet flesh that holds up well when canned.

planting.note: A hybrid that stays tender and holds quality over a wide harvest window.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently. Thin the seedlings so the roots can size up without competing. Pull the round red roots over an extended window, since this hybrid resists getting woody as it grows.

planting.note: Prized for resisting bolting, so it suits early and cool-region sowings.

directions: Sow half an inch deep once the soil warms slightly, taking advantage of this variety's resistance to running to seed. Thin the seedlings to stand a few inches apart. Harvest the smooth, deep-red globes while young and tender for the finest flavor.

planting.note: An heirloom keeper that stays sweet even when the roots grow large.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and give this variety a little extra spacing, since the roots can grow quite large. Keep the soil moist so the flesh stays tender rather than fibrous. Harvest the roots even when big, and store them in a cool, humid place where they keep for months.

planting.note: An early flattened-globe heirloom good for the first spring planting.

directions: Sow seed clusters half an inch deep in cool, workable soil and keep the bed evenly moist. Thin the seedlings promptly so the flattened roots develop smoothly. Harvest early while the dark-red flesh is fine-textured and mild.

planting.note: A pure-white beet that will not stain hands or cutting boards.

directions: Plant seed half an inch deep and keep the soil consistently damp during germination. Thin to one seedling every few inches so the white roots round out. Harvest at two to three inches for very sweet flesh that stays snowy white when cooked.

planting.note: A sweet white variety with reliable, even germination for its color class.

directions: Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and water gently to keep the surface from crusting. Thin the seedlings so each white root has room to swell. Pull the mild, sugary globes young for salads or roasting without any color bleed.

planting.note: A hybrid noted for high sugar content and smooth, uniform roots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in fertile soil and keep it evenly moist through emergence. Thin the seedlings to space the roots a few inches apart. Harvest the round, dark-red beets at two to three inches for exceptionally sweet, tender flesh.

planting.note: A monogerm type whose single-sprout seed greatly reduces thinning.

directions: Sow the monogerm seeds half an inch deep and spaced a couple of inches apart, since each seed produces just one seedling. Keep the soil moist and thin only lightly if at all. Harvest the smooth red roots young for tender, sweet eating.

planting.note: A hybrid with strong tops and deep-red roots that hold well in the ground.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in loose, fertile soil and water to settle. Thin the seedlings so the roots can round out fully. Harvest the globe-shaped beets at two to three inches, and cut the sturdy greens as a bonus crop.

planting.note: A baby-beet hybrid that stays smooth and sweet when picked small.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin the seedlings a little closer than usual for a baby-beet crop. Keep the soil evenly moist so the roots stay tender. Harvest the uniform little globes at one to two inches for bunching or pickling.

planting.note: A hybrid with slow bolting and sweet, tender baby roots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and keep the bed moist through germination. Thin the seedlings to a few inches apart for full roots or slightly closer for babies. Harvest the smooth, deep-red globes while young for the mildest, sweetest flavor.

planting.note: A long cylindrical beet, similar to Cylindra, that slices into even rounds.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in deep, loose soil so the long roots can develop straight. Thin the seedlings so each has room to elongate. Harvest when the cylindrical roots are six to eight inches long for uniform, easy-to-slice pieces.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; the large white roots need a long season to build sugar.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in deep, loose soil and thin the seedlings to several inches apart. Water steadily through the long growing season so the tapered white roots stay healthy. Harvest in fall before hard freezes, when the roots are large and packed with sugar.

planting.note: An old fodder beet grown for huge roots to feed livestock or store over winter.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep and thin the seedlings generously, since the roots grow very large and sit high out of the ground. Keep the bed weed-free and watered through summer. Lift the massive roots in fall before a hard freeze and cure them for winter storage.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; a vigorous white-stemmed chard that crops all season.

directions: Sow seed clusters about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings to stand a hand's width apart. Cut the large crinkled leaves from the outside as needed and the plant will keep producing new growth from the center. Harvest regularly through summer and into fall, since light frost only sweetens the leaves.

planting.note: A mix producing stems in many colors; as ornamental as it is edible.

directions: Sow the seed about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the young plants several inches apart. Pick the outer leaves once they reach usable size to keep a steady supply coming from the middle. Enjoy the rainbow of yellow, pink, orange, and red stems in the garden and on the plate.

planting.note: A deep-red-stemmed heirloom, also sold as Ruby Red chard.

directions: Sow seed clusters about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin to give each plant room to bush out. Harvest the crimson-stemmed, dark-green leaves from the outside so the crown keeps generating new ones. Cut often through the season, and the plants will hold well into cool fall weather.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant white-stemmed heirloom that produces reliably in warm summers.

directions: Sow seed about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings a hand's width apart. Harvest outer leaves continuously, taking care not to strip the growing point. This variety keeps yielding tender, light-green leaves even during summer heat.

planting.note: A single-color selection with vivid golden-yellow stems.

directions: Sow seed clusters three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the plants several inches apart. Cut the outer stems and leaves as they mature to keep new growth flowing. The bright yellow midribs hold their color well when lightly cooked.

scientific_name: Beta vulgaris subsp. cicla 'Five Color Silverbeet'

planting.note: An Australian heirloom mix with red, pink, orange, yellow, and white stems.

directions: Sow seed about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings a few inches apart. Harvest outer leaves regularly to enjoy the full spread of stem colors and encourage fresh growth. The plants tolerate light frost and often overwinter in mild regions.

planting.note: A leaf beet with slender stems and a mild, spinach-like flavor that seldom bolts.

directions: Sow seed three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings a hand's width apart. Pick the tender leaves young and often as a spinach substitute; frequent cutting keeps new leaves coming. This leaf beet resists bolting in heat and can crop from spring right through to the following spring in mild climates.

planting.note: A selection with bright magenta-pink stems and green leaves.

directions: Sow seed clusters three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the plants a few inches apart. Harvest the outer leaves once they reach usable size, leaving the center to keep producing. The vivid pink stems make it a handsome addition to ornamental beds as well as the kitchen.

planting.note: A single-color chard with glowing orange stems and savoyed green leaves.

directions: Sow seed three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings several inches apart. Cut outer leaves regularly so fresh growth keeps emerging from the crown. The bright orange midribs stay colorful through the season and brighten both the garden and the plate.

planting.note: A novelty selection with candy-striped pink-and-white stems.

directions: Sow seed three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the young plants a few inches apart. Harvest outer leaves as they size up, letting the center keep generating new growth. The pink-and-white striped stems are especially striking when the plants are grown for baby leaves.

planting.note: A compact Italian chard with thick white stems, good for close spacing and baby leaves.

directions: Sow seed three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings a few inches apart, closer than for full-size chard. Harvest the tender, glossy leaves young for a quick, mild crop. Its small stature suits containers and tight rows, and cut plants regrow readily.

planting.note: An old-fashioned early best planted while spring soil is still cool.

directions: Drop cut seed potatoes into a shallow trench about four inches deep and cover them with loose, well-drained soil. Water steadily once sprouts emerge and hill the rows to keep tubers from greening. This tan-skinned heirloom sizes up quickly and is ready to dig by mid summer.

planting.note: Plant early for the fast bulking this purple-skinned variety is known for.

directions: Set seed pieces four inches deep in fertile, loosened ground and keep the bed evenly moist during tuber set. Hill soil around the vigorous vines to shade the crop. Harvest the purple-skinned, white-fleshed tubers young for the best flavor, roughly two and a half months after planting.

planting.note: A very early variety for the first new potatoes of the season.

directions: Plant callused seed potatoes four inches deep in loose soil and hill as the plants develop. Keep watering consistent so the round, pink-eyed tubers stay smooth. Lift them early as tender new potatoes about two months from planting.

planting.note: A traditional first early; chit the tubers indoors before planting.

directions: Sprout the seed tubers in a cool, bright spot for a few weeks, then plant them shoots-up about four inches deep. Earth up the rows as the foliage grows and water in dry spells. This heritage first early gives floury, pear-shaped tubers around three months after planting.

planting.note: Classic first early with yellow, waxy flesh.

directions: Chit the tubers indoors, then plant them four inches deep once frost danger is easing. Draw soil up over the stems as they grow to keep tubers covered. Harvest young for waxy salad potatoes, or leave in the ground a little longer for larger, floury ones.

planting.note: A reliable heritage first early that crops heavily.

directions: Plant sprouted seed potatoes about four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed and space rows two feet apart. Earth up regularly and keep the soil evenly moist as tubers form. Lift the white-skinned, waxy potatoes from early summer once flowering begins.

planting.note: A quick-maturing first early with good disease resistance.

directions: Set chitted tubers four inches deep in fertile soil and hill the rows as shoots push up. Water freely during dry weather to swell the round white tubers. This compact first early is ready to lift in roughly eleven to thirteen weeks.

planting.note: One of the earliest croppers, good for container growing.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep, or start them in a deep tub of compost for an early crop. Keep the plants watered and mound soil or compost over the low, compact foliage. Dig the small waxy tubers about ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: A widely adapted heirloom maincrop that tolerates heat.

directions: Set seed pieces about five inches deep in rows and keep the soil evenly moist as the plants bulk up. Earth up the base of the vines as they grow to keep tubers shaded. Dig the smooth, buff-skinned potatoes after the foliage yellows in late summer or fall.

planting.note: The classic late baking and frying russet; needs a long season.

directions: Plant seed potatoes five inches deep in deep, loose soil and give them steady water through a long growing season. Hill the vigorous vines repeatedly and feed midway to size up the netted-brown tubers. Harvest after the tops die back for classic baking and frying potatoes that store for months.

planting.note: A red-skinned maincrop prized for drought tolerance.

directions: Plant seed tubers five inches deep and space rows generously to accommodate the spreading vines. Earth up as the plants grow and keep water steady, though this variety copes better than most with dry spells. Lift the red-skinned, yellow-fleshed potatoes once the tops die down in fall.

planting.note: A floury maincrop favored for chips and roasting.

directions: Set seed potatoes five inches deep in fertile soil and space plants about fifteen inches apart in the row. Hill up steadily and water well during tuber formation to reduce common scab. Harvest the cream-skinned, floury tubers after the haulm dies back for roasting and frying.

planting.note: A famous old floury maincrop with pink-splashed skin.

directions: Plant chitted seed tubers about five inches deep and earth up the rows as growth advances. Keep the bed weeded and watered so the tubers develop evenly. Dig these floury, cream-fleshed potatoes in early autumn once the foliage has collapsed.

planting.note: A vigorous late maincrop with good blight resistance.

directions: Give this strong grower plenty of room, planting seed tubers five inches deep and rows about thirty inches apart. Hill up the sprawling vines and water in dry periods to swell the large tubers. Harvest the white, pink-eyed potatoes late in the season for baking and mash.

planting.note: A late-maincrop with rich, buttery yellow flesh.

directions: Plant seed pieces five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and hill the rows as vines lengthen. Water consistently to keep the golden tubers smooth and blocky. Let the tops die back fully, then dig this excellent keeper for winter storage.
